pattern: Meetings at the exact same timestamp can chain together within that instant, so the secret spreads through whole connected components formed at each timestamp, not just pairwise -- process timestamps in order and grow a knowledge set one time-slice at a time.
hints: Meetings only let the secret travel forward in time, and simultaneous meetings can relay it within that same instant -- so grouping meetings by timestamp matters more than looking at them one at a time.

For each distinct timestamp (processed in increasing order), build the connected components formed by that timestamp's meetings alone -- a person who already knows the secret spreads it to everyone else in their component at that instant.

You don't need one giant graph across all time -- reset the connectivity structure fresh for each timestamp, union the people meeting at that instant, then mark an entire component as informed if any single member of it already knew the secret before this timestamp.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, secret spreads through a chain of timestamps
statement example, one person never learns the secret because their meeting happens before they could know it
statement example, simultaneous meetings relay the secret within the same instant
two people, a single meeting between them
meetings given out of chronological order in the input
isolated person who never meets anyone, remains uninformed
a pair meets amongst themselves at one timestamp and again separately at another, but never meets anyone who knows the secret
duplicate meetings at the same timestamp between the same pair

stress.description: random meeting graphs over a chain of increasing timestamps to exercise large multi-person components
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 1000))
    first_person = rng.randint(1, n - 1)
    num_meetings = rng.randint(1, min(3000, n * 3))
    meetings = []
    for _ in range(num_meetings):
        x = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        y = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        while y == x:
            y = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        t = rng.randint(1, num_meetings)
        meetings.append([x, y, t])
    return [n, meetings, first_person]
